sentences.text: Cheese or sex, which one you want?
Magda Klein.
M-A-G-D-A K-L-E-I-N.
The birth, my name was Magda Czigler, M-A-G-D-A C-Z-I-G-L-E-R.
I was born October the 2nd, 1927, in Mandok, Hungary.
Mandok, M-A-N-D-O-K, Hungary, H-U-N-G-A-R-Y.
I'm 68 and 1/2.
[LAUGHS]
We were seven children.
And I had my older brother and my younger brother.
The rest of us were girls.
And we were a religious family, I would say Orthodox family.
As young girls, we weren't allowed to read Hungarian books.
We had to read only Hebrew books.
And we went to school.
We were having a normal life.
My mother name was Ilona Czigler.
And my father name was Ferenc Czigler.
My father had a big grocery store when the economy was good and the situation was good.
Later on, they took the permit away from us and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] being that we were seven children, so the shoes had to be fixed and we had to have something to walk on.
So he used to go to the shoemaker, sit there, because he had all the time, and watch how they were working on the shoes.
He learned the trade by watching them and he was making the shoes for us.
Beside that, he was making shoes for some other people.
And there were times when it was difficult to obtain the material, like the leather and the nails and all that.
And also, some of the leathers were given on-- on ration, on ticket.
So you had to purchase the ticket from somebody in order to have a pair of shoes and so on.
So I mean, it was a lot of difficulties to obtain material, leather, for a pair of shoes.
As a matter of fact, my grandparents had a-- a machine that was-- was making the wheat.
You needed a big, big heavy leather to go around the wheel.
Now, from that leather, he was using it for the sole, to make the shoe sole.

And he was making shoes for my cousins and many of the family members from his side of the family, because my mother family were most of them in the United State, except one of her younger sister, who was in Hungary.
But the rest of the sisters they-- and brothers, they were all in United State.
Mandok was a fairly middle-sized city.
We had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had like mayor's building.

We had like other big official's building, like some soldiers who examining the tobacco-- I mean, I can't translate-- but who were strictly out for the unofficial-- the bootlegging whiskey, and also for the tobacco.
And there was another building, the gendarmes, the police department, so I would say that it was a fairly middle-class--
The largest was Kisvarda.
Which was--
Which was about 18 kilometers.
Yeah.
As a matter of fact, Kisvarda was the one where I was going to middle class school, [NON-ENGLISH], a gymnasium, whatever you want to call it.
Well, my brother, my older brother was born in 1923.
His name was Philip.
And he used to go to yeshiva until the situation changed.
Then my parents arranged for him to become apprentice at a tailor place.
In other words, he was working three years without any pay.
And after that, he had to have a release, a paper, and make a test front of a committee in order to get the-- sort of like a diploma, that he successfully finished the-- the trade.
And my older sister, she was helping around the house until the situation got bad.
And then she got also a place where she was apprenticed to be a dressmaker.
Her name was [?
Klara, ?] and she was born in '25.
And then I came next.
And I used to help around the house.
But then later on, when the situation got bad, I learned how to spin angora.
This is like the-- we were raising the-- my brother was raising the angora, the-- the rabbit.
And he would shave the rabbit.
And also, we would buy angora from other people, because that was the big in things with the Jewish population.
And then after I was spinning it, we would color it.
And we would make-- my mother was an excellent craft or handworker, or whatever you want to call.
She would knit.
She would sew.
She would crochet whatever you want.
And she would make hats for us.
She would make gloves.
She would make scarves.
She would make sweaters for us.
I mean, naturally, we were helping also.
And sometimes I would go and help in any other way.
Then my younger sister, Elizabeth, she was born in 1930.
So while I was going to school, she was doing some of the chores around the house.
And she was going to school.
Then I had my younger-- my other sister, Eva.
She was born in '33.
AND then my other sister, Martha, she was born in '33-- '35, I think, '35.
And she was such a cutie pie.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
Yeah.
It was my younger brother, Charlie.
[CRYING] [DRINKS] He was born in '38.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the seven children.
Yeah.
We had our own house.
As a matter of fact, we had a very, very beautiful big garden, but beautiful.

We had every type of tree that you could think that was planted in that garden, from gooseberry to raspberry to at least five different type of peaches, apricot, the plums, at least six, seven different kind of plums.
We had apples, the real Jonathan apple, big like this, that you could shine it and see you face in it.
Then we had pears, six, seven type of pears tree.
We had-- we had walnut tree.
We had grape vines, that many times during the Sukkot holiday, my father would just put some cornstalks around that and grapes were the cover.
And we had the sukkah under it.
We put the table in, and that's where we celebrated.
And--
Oh, yes.
We-- we had lot of fun.
First of all, Friday night, after my parents-- my father and my brother came home from shul, we had our kiddish.
And then we had our dinner.
And being that it wasn't a big city, the windows were open.
Our opposite neighbor across the street was also Jewish, so when the dinner was over and they would sing the [NON-ENGLISH], so they were sort of like competing with each other.
By the time one finished, then the other was started.
And sometimes I think about it.
And I was thinking about that during the [INAUDIBLE], I said, my god, what a happy, happy, content life it was.
And after that--
It was comparable large Jewish community.
We had a beautiful shul.
We had a school where it was a Jewish school.
I mean, they didn't call it Jewish school.
They call it Israeli Elementary School.
That was the name of the school, because in our place, they had a Catholic school and they had a Protestant school.
Same thing, they had the church, the Protestant Church and the Catholic church.
As a matter of fact, as a little girl, during the war I had to go up to the steeple of the Protestant church.
And they gave me a binocular that we should watch if the airplanes are coming, not that it would do any good.
We didn't have the telephone up there.
We didn't have any way, except we would holler that the plane is coming.
The plane is coming.
You know, sometimes I was thinking of it that there was a movie, that the plane is coming, the plane.
So they sent me up there to see if the plane is coming.
And to go back to the holidays, we had Rosh Hashanah, which was a beautiful holiday.
The whole family went to shul.
Yom Kippur, the whole family.
When you were at least the age of 10, you were supposed to fast all day long, if not a half a day.
And there were people who would faint in the shul from the fasting, so they would use this Quincy apple, put under the nose or something like that.
Then we had-- as I mentioned before, we had Sukkot.
We had Purim.
My brother would dress up in a woman clothes.
And he really looked very funny.
We were laughing.
We had lot of funs.
And he would go from house to house and he would say the Purim spiel.
And he would come home with some coins that he had gotten.
Our-- our mother would send the [NON-ENGLISH] to another friend of hers with all the goodies.
I mean, this was the custom, the real European custom, not the American.
In America, they do it only in Borough Park and Williamsburg.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
My mother had a very, very good friend whom she went to school together.
And her children were very good friend with us.
And also, we had-- we had I would say mostly Jewish friend, because somehow, the non-Jews, they didn't want-- they didn't associate themselves with the Jews.
Later on, when they became like teenagers, in the age of let's say 16, 17, then they would go to dancing school together and they became friends.
But as children, you-- you didn't associate too much.
The school was a very nice general elementary school.
We were six grade in one building, one room.
And sometimes I think about that, how amazing it is that one teacher was able to teach six grade in the same classroom.
And there were no turmoil and there were no interference and each one learned the ABC and each one learned how to read.

And when it was the time that we were like in third grade, fourth grade, you had to stand back to the map with a stick showing when he asked where is this state, where is that state, where is this river, where is that river.
And sometimes it amazes me to-- to put you through such a drill.
And after that, I went to the middle school, the gymnasium, which was far away.
I had to take a train.
The train station was very far away from our home.
But I went.
And during the winter, sometimes the snow drifts were so big I fell into it and I thought I'd never climb out of it, but I did.
And we got there, frozen off, so we went to the train station and we went there.
I had my father brother, my uncle, living not far from the train station.

And they had a daughter the same age as I am, so we stayed there until it was time to go to school, because we would have to get out-- get out of the house 6 o'clock to take the train and the school started 8 o'clock, so we had time to spend there.
And then we would walk with my cousin to school.
This was all very nice.
And then in the-- '38, there started to get a lot of commotion there, with like the black-dressed soldiers.
I don't know where they came from.
But they had-- going around and asking every Jewish family that they should donate money, because they want to erect a flagpole at middle of the marketplace.
And if you didn't give enough money, then they weren't satisfied.

And there were times when they would take Jewish men up into the-- to the city hall and they would question and they would beat them and so on, because he didn't give enough money towards the flagpole.
And then--
They were [INTERPOSING VOICES] Hungarians.
They were the hoodlums that-- that they-- they wore the black uniform.
The-- that was the beginning of the-- of the anti-Semitism.
That was the beginning of the-- of the-- I-- I can't even remember the name of it, how you-- in Germany, they called it the-- the youth group.
Here, I don't know what [INAUDIBLE].
They weren't young people.
They were hoodlums who didn't have a job.
And they were just out to get the Jews.
That was the year that they invaded Czechoslovakia, because they felt that the Hungarians are liberating them.
Meantime, they had a better time than the Hungarians had.
They were more modern, more up to date.
They had different factories that the people benefited by it.
And we Hungarians, we didn't have that.
Then as the time was going by, it always got more and more deteriorated.
They were coming out with all different law that the Jews weren't supposed to have this, weren't supposed to have that.
And also, they started to draft many of the young capable man for working battalion.
Previous to that, when a man, a young man reached the age of 21, he was drafted to the Army.
But after '38, there was very limited cases.
Most of them, they were called in to the working battalion.
My brother Philip wasn't in that age yet.
My brother Philip was in the age-- he was 21 in '44.
And he was-- he received a letter that he has to report.
And that was right after Passover.
And he went to the train station.
My mother picked him up, everything.
We-- we prepared him with clothing and so on.
And he came back after that and he said they wouldn't let him go.
That was in '44.
But from '38 until '44, things was always deteriorating more and more.
It was getting worse and worst.
You know, Ellen, they didn't have to talk about it, because when you walk down the street, the other kids would say, you dirty Jew, you filthy Jew, you this, you that.
And there was one occasion when one of the goyim, a boy, called my brother all these names.
So I was a big shot.
I went over and I gave him a slug that his nose started to bleed.
And my brother said, why did you do it?
He going to kill you.
And next time, you won't be with me.
Then he will-- I said, I will-- I will hit him.
I will-- I will take care of myself.
I was shaking inside, but I was a big shot, you know.
And I was afraid of that boy.
Whenever I would see him, I would always try to avoid him, because I felt that he going to give me a slap that my nose start to bleed too.
But somehow, he never did it.
Seems to me he-- he was a much finer person.
[LAUGHS]
There was a time when we received a letter that we have to prove that we are Hungarian citizens from way back from our great-great-grandparent.
Now, if you lived in a modern city, then they had all the registration of the birth certificate and so on.
But in those days, they didn't had registration for the birth.
So naturally, my grandparents, my great-grandparents had no-- no birth certificates, so we couldn't produce it.
There were witnesses who says that, yes, we remember them, we know them, they lived here, but no birth certificate.
So being that we didn't have the birth-- birth certificate for my great-great-grandparent, so my mother also was a very brave, outspoken person.
So the-- the mayor said that if you don't have all these proper papers, then we're going to deport you.
So my mother said, go ahead.
If you want to deport us and you want to support nine people, go ahead.
Little did we know now that the place Kistarcsa where they wanted to deport us, they were a terrible concentration place, where from there, they were taking the people to Auschwitz.
But that time, we weren't aware of it.
We didn't know it.
So what happened is that we know somebody who was very good friends with this mayor.
And we gave some money and the questionnaire disappeared, the paper disappeared.
Everything disappeared, and we were left in peace.
There were times when they took away the-- the permit from every Jew, because they felt that the Jew is not entitled to have a permit.
If you were a businessman, any kind of businessman, they took the permit away.
They called in all the young men for the working battalion.
They took away the permit to make a living.
Meantime, the whole population was put on ration.
Soap you couldn't get.
Flour and fat, you know, whatever it is.
So meantime-- and the fat part didn't affected us.
Because we were raising all kinds of domestic animals, like goose, the ducks, and chickens, so we had the egg supply.
We had the potatoes from the garden.
We had the corn and we had all the fruit.
My mother would preserve the fruit.
She would make-- preserve the pickles and cabbage and all that.
So when it came to food, we were pretty well taken care of from our land, what we had.
But you still needed soap and you still needed clothing to wear, and is not that easy.
When-- when your hand is tired, you can't move it.
It's very, very difficult.
But with money, you were able to get it.
You were able to get around with it.
And there were times that we would fatten up the goose, take it to the [NON-ENGLISH], clean it, and everything kosher.
And then I would take it to a big city, Ungvar.
And when I get off the train, I would go walk.
And as I'm walking, I heard some Jewish spoken people on this street, so I would approach them and I said, look.
I have here this certificate from the-- the [NON-ENGLISH].
This is a kosher goose.
And if you like to buy it, I sell it to you.
So she bought it.
They were very happy, very delighted to-- to buy kosher goose.
And the meantime, the money what I received for it, I was able to buy material to make a dress, to make a shirt, and things like that, so it was like black market-y sort of.
Do you think that your family ever considered leaving Hungary?
It-- it never was mentioned.
It never came to our mind, because what happened is that my mother's sisters and brothers were all in United State.
We were supposed to come in '37.
We had the affidavit.
We had all the papers.
Then my brother was born.
And when my brother was born, we had to send back the papers to have one extra name put on it.
By the time the paper arrived, it was '38 and all the border were closed, so we were stuck in Hungary.
We-- we-- we couldn't think of going anywhere by that time, because we couldn't go.
And it was-- it was a pretty big mark on us, because we were all prepared to come to United State.
Now, it-- it was a lot of difficult years, lot of difficult time being a Jew in-- in those days in Hungary.
And later on, when it got to be '44, then they made an announcement.
Those days they had a drummer who would walk on the middle of the street and he would start to drum.
Now, when he started to drum, then all the people would stand front of their gate or front of their house door and they listened.
And he had a paper in his hand and he would read, 'hear, hear, hear, the government of Hungary' and so and so.

He was phrasing it with such a flowery language that you would think that God now is-- and the Israelite people are not allowed to go on the street without a yellow star and they-- they're not allowed to travel and they're not allowed to go out after dark and so and so.
This took place in the end of-- end of '43, like the end of '43, the beginning of '44.
So we had some yellow material and we cut out some stars.
And we were having fun sewing it on our coat.
And you were supposed to have it on your dress also, but we didn't put it on our dress, because being that it was cold, you needed a coat, so you weren't walking around without a dress.
So that was just the beginning of it.
And then later on, they announced that-- that no Jews are allowed to leave their house.
So we were inside in the house, and we were looking through the gate.
We were looking through the window behind the curtains.
And we had seen that there were this-- a lot of policemen, the gendarmes, they were going from each Jewish house.
And I see that after a while they were coming out with a little bundle in their hands and they were going towards the [? shul ?.] I have to stop now for a tape change.
